source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A long thin uniform bar of mass $M$ and length $L$ is hung from a fixed (assumed frictionless) axis at $A$ as shown in Fig. 1.131. The moment of inertia about $A$ is $M L^{2} / 3$. In general, as a result of $J$, there will be an impulse $J'$ on the bar from the axis at $A$. What is $J'$?

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A long thin uniform bar of mass $M$ and length $L$ is hung from a fixed (assumed frictionless) axis at $A$ as shown in Fig. 1.131. The moment of inertia about $A$ is $M L^{2} / 3$. In general, as a result of $J$, there will be an impulse $J'$ on the bar from the axis at $A$. What is $J'$?
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL image object representation rather than a concrete file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: The initial velocity of the center of mass of the bar is $v=\frac{\omega L}{2}$. So the change in the momentum of the bar is $M v=\frac{M \omega L}{2}$. As this is equal to the total impulse on the bar, we have $J+J'=\frac{M \omega L}{2}$. Hence $J'=\frac{M \omega L}{2}-J=J\left(\frac{3a}{2L}-1\right)$.

problem_main_record.reasoning_chain: The initial velocity of the center of mass of the bar is $v=\frac{\omega L}{2}$. So the change in the momentum of the bar is $M v=\frac{M \omega L}{2}$. As this is equal to the total impulse on the bar, we have $J+J'=\frac{M \omega L}{2}$. Hence $J'=\frac{M \omega L}{2}-J=J\left(\frac{3a}{2L}-1\right)$.
